production_pipeline.title: Show Bible & IP Foundation
tasks: Expand each character into a 10-page character bible (voice, arc, relationships, powers spec)
Write the season 1 series bible — world, tone, visual language, overarching mythology
Define the villain hierarchy and the organization's backstory
Lock the 12 story-arc outline (beat sheet per arc)
Register the IP — show name, characters, and any original lore as copyright
production_pipeline.executive_note: This is the document you pitch from. Investors, distributors, and collaborators all want to see a bible before anything else. Do this first.

production_pipeline.title: Visual Development
tasks: Commission character design sheets for all 5 heroes (front, side, 3/4 view + power states)
Define the AI art style guide — reference images, color palettes, prohibited styles
Create 3–5 environment concept boards (Meridian City skyline, HQ, villain base)
Select and lock your AI image pipeline: Midjourney for concept, Runway/Kling for animation
Produce a 60–90 second style proof — a short clip to show exactly what the show looks like
production_pipeline.executive_note: The style proof is your most powerful pitch tool. A single beautiful minute beats 40 slides.

production_pipeline.title: Pilot Production (Episode 1)
tasks: Write the full Episode 1 script — scene-by-scene with dialogue
Create storyboards or AI-generated animatics for every scene
Cast and record voice actors (SAG or indie — define your budget tier)
Generate all animation sequences using your AI pipeline, scene by scene
Sound design, score, and mix — audio is 50% of animation quality
Deliver a complete, polished Episode 1 as your proof of concept
production_pipeline.executive_note: The pilot is your calling card. Everything else is a promise — the pilot is evidence.

production_pipeline.title: Distribution & Monetization Strategy
tasks: Define your release platform: YouTube, streaming pitch, or direct IP sale
Build a pre-release audience strategy — character reveal reels, behind-the-scenes AI content
Identify potential licensing partners for merchandise
Explore distribution pitches: Netflix Animation, Cartoon Network, Amazon Kids+, Apple TV+
Alternative: YouTube-first with brand deal / Patreon model to self-fund season 1
production_pipeline.executive_note: A finished pilot gives you leverage. You can self-distribute to build an audience or use it to shop to streamers — but you need the pilot first.

production_pipeline.title: Season 1 Full Production
tasks: Write all ~48 episode scripts (12 story arcs) with a dedicated writers room (2–3 writers minimum)
Build a repeatable AI production pipeline to generate episodes efficiently
Establish a release cadence — weekly drops build audiences fastest
Lock music licensing or commission an original score
Plan the Season 1 finale event — make the final arc a cultural moment
production_pipeline.executive_note: Efficiency is everything here. Once your pipeline is dialed in from the pilot, each episode should take less time to produce than the last.

executive_priorities.label: Immediate Action
executive_priorities.heading: Write the show bible before anything else
executive_priorities.body: Every creative decision downstream depends on having a locked document that defines the show. Characters, tone, world rules, villain mythology, season arc — all of it on paper. This is also what you hand to any collaborator, investor, or distributor. Without it, you're building on sand.

executive_priorities.label: Creative Foundation
executive_priorities.heading: Produce the style proof before pitching to anyone
executive_priorities.body: Streaming platforms and investors are pitched AI animated shows every week now. The ones that get greenlit have visual proof. A 60–90 second clip showing your art style, your characters in motion, and your tone is worth more than any deck. Build this before you go to any meeting.

executive_priorities.label: IP & Legal
executive_priorities.heading: Protect your intellectual property immediately
executive_priorities.body: Register the show name, characters, and series bible as original copyright. Consult an entertainment attorney about work-for-hire agreements before bringing on any creative collaborators. This is a franchise-scale property — treat it like one from day one.

executive_priorities.label: Platform Strategy
executive_priorities.heading: Decide: Self-distribute first or pitch to streamers
executive_priorities.body: There are two viable paths. Path A: launch on YouTube to build a fanbase organically, monetize through ads and merchandise, use audience numbers as leverage for a streaming deal. Path B: produce the pilot and pitch directly to Netflix Animation, Cartoon Network, or Amazon Kids+ before public release. Path A has lower ceiling but lower risk. Path B is higher stakes but could move faster.

executive_priorities.label: The Teen Titans Playbook
executive_priorities.heading: Merchandise is where the real money lives
executive_priorities.body: Teen Titans wasn't just a show — it was a $400M+ merchandise empire. Design your characters from the start with product in mind. Each hero needs a signature color, a distinctive silhouette, and an iconic accessory. Those three things are what make a plush toy sell. The visual design you lock now will determine your licensing ceiling for the next decade.

executive_priorities.label: The North Star
executive_priorities.heading: Your 18-month goal: Season 1 complete, audience established
executive_priorities.body: Twelve story arcs (~48 episodes). A fanbase. One cultural moment — a scene, a villain reveal, a character death — that people talk about. If you hit those three things by the end of Season 1, you have a show. Everything else is execution.
